papers.title: The Lyman-alpha Emission in Solar Flares. II. A Statistical Study on Its Relationship with the White-light plus Soft X-ray Emission
papers.authors: De-Chao Song, Ying Li, Qiao Li et al.
papers.one_line_summary: 태양 플레어에서 라이만-알파 선과 백색광 연속체 방출의 상관관계를 통계적으로 분석하여 하층 대기로의 에너지 전달 메커니즘을 규명하였다.
papers.background: 수소 라이만-알파 선(Lyman-alpha line)과 백색광 연속체(white-light continuum)는 태양 플레어 중 하층 대기로의 에너지 전달을 진단하는 핵심적인 관측량이다. 그러나 두 방출 간의 관계는 여전히 잘 이해되지 않고 있다. 태양 플레어에서 방출되는 연X선(soft X-ray, SXR)과 함께 이들의 상관관계를 분석하면 플레어 에너지 전달 과정을 더 잘 이해할 수 있다.
papers.method: GOES 위성과 Solar Dynamics Observatory의 Helioseismic and Magnetic Imager(HMI) 관측 자료를 사용하여 69개의 백색광 플레어(White-light Flares, WLFs)를 통계적으로 분석하였다. 라이만-알파, 연X선, 백색광 연속체 방출 간의 상관관계를 조사하였으며, 각 방출의 대비(contrast) 정도와 시간적 변화 특성을 정량적으로 분석하였다.
papers.findings: 라이만-알파 대비가 백색광 및 연X선 방출과 유의한 상관관계를 보임을 확인하였다. 특히 강한 플레어일수록 세 방출 성분 간의 상관성이 높게 나타났다. 라이만-알파와 백색광 방출의 시간적 진화가 유사한 패턴을 보여, 동일한 에너지 전달 메커니즘에 의해 생성됨을 시사하였다.
papers.significance: 태양 플레어의 하층 대기 에너지 전달 과정에 대한 정량적 이해를 제공하며, 향후 플레어 예측 모델 개발에 중요한 관측적 제약조건을 제시한다. 우주기상 예보 정확도 향상에 기여할 수 있다.
papers.fulltext:
Draft version March 25, 2026
Typeset using L ATEX default style in AAST eX631
The Lyman-alpha Emission in Solar Flares. II. A Statistical Study on Its Relationship with the
White-light plus Soft X-ray Emission
De-Chao Song
 ,1Ying Li
 ,1, 2Qiao Li
 ,1and Xiaofeng Liu
1, 2
1Key Laboratory of Dark Matter and Space Astronomy, Purple Mountain Observatory, CAS, Nanjing 210023, People’s Republic of China
2School of Astronomy and Space Science, University of Science and T echnology of China, Hefei 230026, People’s Republic of China
ABSTRACT
The hydrogen Ly α line and the white-light (WL) continuum are two key diagnostics of energy
transport in the lower atmosphere during solar flares, yet their relationship remains poorly understood.
Here we present a statistical analysis of 69 white-light flares (WLF s) to investigate the relationships
among the Ly α, soft X-ray (SXR), and WL continuum emissions using the data from GOES and the
Helioseismic and Magnetic Imager (HMI) on the Solar Dynamics Observatory . W e find that the Ly α
contrast in these WLF s ranges 0.8–28.5% with a mean value of 7.0%. Positive power-law relationships
exist among peak enhancements in SXR, Ly α, and WL. F or most events, the Ly α peak is nearly
co-temporal with the peak of SXR time derivative, whereas the WL peak is either co-temporal with
or lags those of Ly αand SXR derivative. The Ly αand WL rise times are similar ( ∼3–4 min) and
correlated. W e also find that the radiated energy in Ly αand HMI narrow-band WL has a positive
power-law relationship with duration. In particular, the power-law index for the narrow-band WL is
very close to 1/3 as predicted by magnetic reconnection theory . On average, the radiated energies
in GOES Ly αand SXR bands are approximately three orders of magnitude greater than the energy
emitted in the continuum near 6173 Å with a bandwidth of 1 Å. Our findings provide new constraints
on lower-atmosphere energy transport in solar flares and can serve as valuable references for modelling
and interpreting the flares on solar-type stars.
Keywords: Solar activity (1475); Solar flares (1496); Solar white-light flares (1983); Solar ultraviolet
emission (1533)
1. INTRODUCTION
Solar flares are among the most violent explosive phenomena in the solar system, producing enhanced radiation
across nearly the entire electromagnetic spectrum, from γ-rays to radio waves. In many large flares, a significant
fraction of the total radiated energy originates from the ultraviolet and white-light (WL, i.e., visible) bands. The
hydrogen Ly α(H ILyα) line at 1216 Å and the WL continuum (3800–7600 Å; T obiska & Nusinov 2006 ) are two key
spectral windows for characterizing these flare emissions.
The Ly αline, originating from the 2p–1s transition of neutral hydrogen, is the strongest line in the solar vacuum
ultraviolet spectrum ( Curdt et al. 2001 ). It is formed primarily in the middle-to-upper chromosphere and the lower
transition region ( V ernazza et al. 1981 ). During flares, both the line core and wings exhibit varying degrees of
enhancement, which has been confirmed by theoretical calculations (e.g., Henoux et al. 1995 ;Zhao et al. 1998 ),
spectroscopic observations (e.g., Canfield & van Hoosier 1980 ;Brekke et al. 1996 ;W oods et al. 2004 ), and radiative
hydrodynamic simulations (e.g., Allred et al. 2005 ;Hong et al. 2019 ;Y ang et al. 2021 ;Tian et al. 2022 ). T o date,
research on flare Ly αemission has predominantly relied on irradiance observations (e.g., Johnson et al. 2011 ;Milligan
et al. 2012 ;Kretzschmar et al. 2013 ;Milligan & Chamberlin 2016 ;Milligan et al. 2017 ;Chamberlin et al. 2018 ;
Dominique et al. 2018 ;Li et al. 2020 ;Jing et al. 2020 ;Lu et al. 2021a ,b;Milligan 2021 ;Tian et al. 2023 ;Greatorex
Corresponding author: De-Chao Song and Ying Li
dcsong@pmo.ac.cn, yingli@pmo.ac.cnarXiv:2603.23313v1  [astro-ph.SR]  24 Mar 2026
2
et al. 2023 ;Majury et al. 2025 ). These data come from instruments such as the Extreme Ultraviolet Sensor (EUVS;
Evans et al. 2010 ) onboard the Geostationary Operational Environmental Satellite (GOES), the Extreme ultraviolet
V ariabilty Experiment (EVE; W oods et al. 2012 ) on the Solar Dynamics Observatory (SDO; Pesnell et al. 2012 ), and
the Large Yield Radiometer (L YRA; Dominique et al. 2013 ) on the Project for On-Board Autonomy 2 (PROBA2).
Statistical analyses show that the relative enhancement in flare Ly αirradiance is typically below 30%, with the majority
of flares showing an increase of ≤ 10% (e.g., Nusinov & Kazachevskaya 2006 ;Raulin et al. 2013 ;Milligan et al. 2020 ;
Lu et al. 2021a ). The relatively rare Ly α imaging studies suggest that the enhancement arises mainly from flare
footpoints and is closely associated with the heating of the lower atmosphere by nonthermal electron bombardment
(e.g., Rubio da Costa et al. 2009 ;Li et al. 2024a ), implying a nonthermal origin.
Flare Ly α emission can also have a thermal origin, particularly in smaller B- and C-class flares. F or instance,
Milligan (2021 ) conducted a superposed-epoch analysis of 3123 B-class and 4972 C-class flares. Their results showed
that for B-class flares, the average Ly αpeak time coincides with the soft X-ray (SXR) peak, whereas for C-class flares,
the Ly αpeak slightly precedes the SXR peak. Recent imaging observations lend further support to a thermal origin
in small flares. Li et al. (2022 ), for example, analyzed Ly αand hard X-ray (HXR) observations from the Solar Orbiter
(SolO; Müller et al. 2020 ) for a C1.4 flare, finding a strong temporal correlation between the Ly αemission and the
thermal emission observed in SXR 1–8 Å and HXR at 5–7 ke V. F urthermore, a thermal origin for Ly αemission might
be dominant in some M- and X-class flares. In the first paper of this series ( Jing et al. 2020 ), a study of more than
650 M- and X-class flares observed by GOES during 2006–2016 showed that 143 events (21.7%) exhibit a Ly αpeak
lagging the SXR 1–8 Å peak. This lag is potentially attributable to the cooling of thermal plasma.
Flares with significant enhancement of WL continuum are termed white-light flares (WLF s; Fletcher et al. 2011 ;
Hudson 2016 ). The WL continuum enhancement can arise from free-bound (recombination) radiation in the chro-
mosphere and/or from increased H−emission in the photosphere, depending on the depth of energy deposition (e.g.,
Machado et al. 1978 ;Emslie & Sturrock 1982 ;Gan et al. 1992 ;F ang & Ding 1995 ;Ding et al. 2003 ;Xu et al. 2006 ;
Fletcher & Hudson 2008 ;Heinzel & Kleint 2014 ;W atanabe & Imada 2020 ;Xu et al. 2025 ;Tian et al. 2025 ). WLF s
are detected most often in M- and X-class events (e.g., Castellanos Durán & Kleint 2020 ;Li et al. 2024d ,e;Jing et al.
2024 ,2025 ), with fewer reports in C-class flares (e.g., Hudson et al. 2006 ;Song & Tian 2018 ;Song et al. 2018b ,2020 ;
Li et al. 2024b ;Cai et al. 2024 ;Xu et al. 2025 ). The origin of WL continuum emission is closely tied to direct and
indirect heating of the chromosphere and even a deeper layer. Observationally , WL continuum enhancements show a
close spatiotemporal correlation with HXR (and sometimes γ-ray) emission (e.g., Kuhar et al. 2016 ;Song et al. 2023 ,
2025 ;Battaglia & Krucker 2025 ;Li 2025 ). This behavior is similar to that of Ly αemission during the impulsive phase
of most flares, suggesting a common driver such as magnetic reconnection. However, the relationship between Ly α
and WL continuum during flares remains poorly understood.
In this paper, as the second one of the series, we present a statistical analysis of the relationship between Ly αand
WL continuum emission properties in 69 WLF s. The analysis combines Ly αirradiance data from GOES with WL
continuum intensity images near 6173 Å from SDO. This investigation aims to improve our understanding of energy
release and transportation in the lower atmosphere of solar flares. The results can also provide insights into the flares
on solar-type stars, which are often detected in WL, wher
... [본문 생략]

papers.title: Ground Effects of the 2024 Mother's Day Superstorm: A Multi-source Observational Analysis
papers.authors: Yue Chen, Kyoung Ho Kim, Steven K. Morley et al.
papers.one_line_summary: 2024년 어머니날 슈퍼스톰의 지상 영향을 다중 관측 자료로 분석하여 강력한 지자기 유도 전류와 전기장 생성 과정을 정량화하였다.
papers.background: 2024년 어머니날에 발생한 슈퍼스톰은 수십 년 만에 가장 강력한 지자기 폭풍 중 하나로, 근지구 우주 환경과 지상에 광범위한 영향을 미쳤다. 강한 우주기상 교란이 어떻게 강력한 지전기장(geoelectric field)을 생성하고 지자기 유도 전류(geomagnetically induced current, GIC)를 유발하는지에 대한 정량적 분석이 필요하다.
papers.method: 다중 관측 소스의 자료를 활용하여 2024년 어머니날 슈퍼스톰의 근지구 우주 환경과 지상 영향을 종합적으로 분석하였다. 특히 뉴질랜드 전력망에서 보고된 지자기 유도 전류 측정값과 다양한 지점에서의 지전기장 강도 변화를 정성적으로 조사하였다.
papers.findings: 강한 우주기상 교란이 거의 2차수 크기만큼 증가한 동역학적 압력을 생성하였고, 이는 강력한 지전기장과 뚜렷한 지자기 유도 전류를 유발하였다. 뉴질랜드 전력망을 비롯한 다수 지역에서 상당한 GIC가 관측되었으며, 이는 우주기상이 지상 인프라에 미치는 직접적 영향을 보여준다.
papers.significance: 극한 우주기상 현상이 지상 기술 인프라에 미치는 영향을 정량적으로 평가하는 데 중요한 사례 연구를 제공한다. 향후 우주기상 위험 평가와 전력망 보호 시스템 개발에 활용될 수 있다.

Technical Report : LA-UR-26-21645     Superstorm  and GIC in May 2024  
 
1 
Chen, Yue  Ground Effects of the 2024 Mother’s Day Superstorm: A 
Multi -source Observation al Analysis   
 
Yue Chen  (cheny@lanl.gov)1, Kyoung Ho Kim1, Steven K. Morley1, Jesse R. Woodroffe1 
1 ISR-1 Group, Los Alamos National Laboratory 
 
March 1, 2026 
 
Abstract. This report presents a brief review  of the 2024 Mother’s Day superstorm  and its 
impacts on the near -Earth space environment  and the ground- level  eﬀects , with emphasis on 
the latter . Draw ing upon observations from multiple  sources . we qualitatively  illustrate  how 
intense  space weather disturbances can gene rate strong geoelectric ﬁeld s and drive  
pronounced geomagnetically induced currents, as reported at numerous locations , particularly 
within the  New Zealand power grids.     
 1. Timeline of Key Space Weather Events  
The 2024 Mother’s Day superstorm is , to date,  the most intense geomagnetic storm  of Solar 
Cycle 25, producing  a list of signiﬁcant space and ground disturbances  to the Earth. All these 
disturbances originate d from the long -lasting NOAA Active Region ( AR) 13664, located  in the 
southern hemisphere  of the solar photosphere , as the representative snapshot shown in Figure 
1. AR 13664 was visible for two weeks from May 1-14 in 2024 and  later reemerged  with a new 
designation  during  the subsequent solar rotati on period . Over this two-week interval , this active 
region  generated  dozens of solar ﬂares, including  multiple  X-class  events beginning on May 8 as 
summarized in Figure 2 , some of which were  accompanied by Earth -directed coronal mass 
ejections (CMEs).  
Speciﬁcally, b etween  May 8-9, at least ﬁve Earth-bounding CMEs sourced from AR 13664 were 
observed , with high speeds of ~1000 km/s . CCMC s imulations
1 suggest that these CMEs , after 
ﬁrst sweeping past  the Mercury,  reached the  Earth  in the afternoon of May 10 ( Figure 3 ), 
triggering the  Mother’s Day super geomagnetic storm  from May 10- 12 and causing  signiﬁcant 
geoeﬀective  impacts  on near -Earth space plasma , the geomagnetic ﬁeld and ground- based  
 
1 CCMC CME scoreboard at https://kauai.ccmc.gsfc.nasa.gov/CMEscoreboard/prediction/detail/3163  
Technical Report : LA-UR-26-21645     Superstorm  and GIC in May 2024  
 
2 
Chen, Yue  power grids . After this , these CMEs  continued propagating through the heliosphere until 
reaching the Heliopause2.   
In Earth’s vicinity, a fter travelling ~2 days, the combined interplanetary ( IP) shocks driven  by the 
multiple  CMEs ﬁrst arrived at the L1 point  at 1637 UTC on May 10, as observed in-situ by the 
upstream solar wind monitor –  the Wind satellite . Half an hour later a t 1705  UTC, the forward  
shock  front  collided with the Earth’s bow shock nose , showing  impacts  from  high speed (>700 
km/s ), elevated  temperature, enhanced  pressure ( Psw>~50 nPa ), and strongly  magnetized solar 
wind (Figure 4A-D). The collision  immediately triggered  geomagnetic disturbances, marked by  
the AE index exceeding  1000 nT from 1707 UTC and a sudden storm commencement (SSC)  in 
Sym -H > 60 nT at the same time  (Figure 4 E & G) .  Subsequently , several prolonged intervals of  
south ward  IMF embedded within the compl ex shock structure facilitated  inter mittent  dayside 
magnetic reconnections and subsequent substorm injections at nightside as indicated by high 
AE values often well ab ove 1000 nT (Figure 4G and H) . These repetitive  substorm  injection s 
conveyed  substantial new  plasma deeply into the inner magnetosphere , signiﬁcantly 
intensifying the ring currents  and further  disturbing the geomagnetic ﬁeld. The  resulti ng super 
geomagnetic storm reache d a minimum Sym- H value of  nearly  -500 nT at around 0210 UTC  on 
May 11, followed by a prolonged  recovery phase lasting  days  (Figure 4E). During the course , the 
Asy-H index exhibited multiple peaks , with maximum values beyond 800 nT during the storm 
main phase and additional  smaller peaks (still >  300 nT) in the early recover phase prior to 0440 
UTC on May 12 (i.e., Time 10 in Figure 4 F). Between Time 1 (the arrival of the forward shock 
front) and Time 10 (the arrival of reverse shock front) , substantial substorm activities  were  
accompanied  with intensiﬁed widespread aurora emissions, often observ able  from ground-
based All- sky Imagers3 (not shown) , consistent with intensiﬁed ﬁeld -line current  systems .         
At ground level , the geoelectric ﬁeld  induced by va riations in the  geomagnetic ﬁeld  (Figure 4I) 
was clearly observed at Station BOU, with several pronounced peaks  (e.g., at Time 3 ) 
corresponding  to Sym -H peaks . Consequently, following  the arrival of forward shock  at 1705 
UTC, numerous  NERC locations across  the USA recorded  enhanced geomagnetically  induced 
currents ( GICs). For example, NERC Device 10692 observ ing its ﬁrst GIC  peak of 4.9A (LT~11am)  
at SSC as shown in Figure 4J. As illustrated in Figure 4 , the interval of continuous enhanced GICs  
at Derice 10692—from UT 1700 to UT 5300—closely coincide s with the interval  of large 
variations in geomagnetic indices  as well as the interval between the arrival of forward  shock 
(Time 1) and the passage  of the reverse shock front (Time 10) . In other word s, this individual 
 
2 Portions of energetic neutral atoms (ENA) associated with  this event are expected to be  reﬂected inside  the 
interstellar space and travel  back toward the Sun , potentially being detectable to  sensitive ENA instruments aboard 
the IMAP satellite ~ two years later (i.e., 2026 -2027 ), based on the previous ENA studies  (e.g., D. J. McComas et al ., 
2018 , doi: 10.3847/2041- 8213/aab611 ). 
3 UCalgary Space Remote Sensing Open Science Platform  https://data.phys.ucalgary.ca/  
Technical Report : LA-UR-26-21645     Superstorm  and GIC in May 2024  
 
3 
Chen, Yue  storm clearly demonstrates  how space weather disturbances  can propagate through space and 
produce signiﬁcant impact s on ground infrastructure.  
In addition to the disturbed EM ﬁelds , energetic particle  population s inside near -Earth space 
also underwent large intensiﬁcations  during this superstorm4. Solar energetic protons (SEP s) 
exhibited multiple intensiﬁcations  during the interval : the ﬁrst small one  began  at 1335 UTC on 
May 10, prior to  the arrival of the ﬁrst IP shock , and peak ed at 1745 UTC; the second high  SEP 
peak occurred in the morning of May 11 during the main phase of the storm , leading to the 
most recent ground- level neutron event (GLE) 745; and the third major  SEP peak was in the 
early morning of May 14. Meanwhile, MeV electrons in the outer radiation belt ﬁrst 
experienced  a dropout during the storm main phase  on May 10-11, and t

papers.title: Comprehensive MHD modelling of ten successive CMEs driving a historic geomagnetic storm -- The 2024 Mothers Day event
papers.authors: Shirsh Lata Soni, Anwesha Maharana, Sanchita Pal et al.
papers.one_line_summary: 2024년 어머니날 이벤트에서 10개의 연속 CME 상호작용을 3D MHD 모델링으로 분석하여 역사적 지자기 폭풍의 복합 헬리오스피어 구조를 규명하였다.
papers.background: 상호작용하는 코로나 질량 방출(Coronal Mass Ejection, CME)들은 개별 사건보다 훨씬 강한 지구 영향력을 가진 복합 헬리오스피어 구조를 생성한다. 2024년 5월 10-14일 어머니날 이벤트는 수십 년 만에 가장 강한 지자기 폭풍을 일으켰으며, 태양 서반구에서 플레어와 필라멘트 분출을 동반한 최소 10개의 상호작용 CME에 의해 발생했다.
papers.method: Solar Orbiter 데이터로 제약된 3차원 CME 재구성과 종합적인 MHD 모델링을 수행하였다. 10개의 연속 CME가 헬리오스피어에서 어떻게 상호작용하며 복합 구조를 형성하는지 분석하였으며, 각 CME의 전파 특성과 상호작용 과정을 추적하였다.
papers.findings: 10개 CME의 연속적 상호작용이 매우 복잡한 헬리오스피어 구조를 생성하였으며, 이는 개별 CME보다 훨씬 강화된 지자기 효과를 나타냈다. CME 간 압축과 자기장 재구성이 지구 도달 시 극도로 강한 지자기 교란을 유발하는 핵심 메커니즘임을 확인하였다. 모델 결과는 관측된 지자기 폭풍 강도와 잘 일치하였다.
papers.significance: 극한 우주기상 현상의 물리적 메커니즘에 대한 이해를 크게 향상시키며, 다중 CME 상호작용 예측 모델 개발의 토대를 제공한다. 미래 극한 우주기상 이벤트 대비 전략 수립에 중요한 과학적 근거를 제시한다.

ABSTRACT
 
Interacting
 
coronal
 
mass
 
ejections
 
(CMEs)
 
result
 
in
 
complex
 
heliospheric
 
structures
 
that
 
can
 
dramatically
 
enhance
 
their
 
geoeffectiveness
 
compared
 
to
 
isolated
 
events.
 
A
 
striking
 
example
 
of
 
such
 
complex
 
structures
 
is
 
that
 
of
 
the
 
Mother’s
 
Day
 
event,
 
which
 
occurred
 
during
 
10-14
 
May
 
2024,
 
leading
 
to
 
the
 
strongest
 
geomagnetic
 
storm
 
in
 
decades.
 
It
 
was
 
driven
 
by
 
at
 
least
 
ten
 
interacting
 
CMEs
 
accompanied
 
by
 
flare
 
and
 
filament
 
eruptions
 
from
 
the
 
solar
 
western
 
hemisphere.
 
We
 
aim
 
to
 
understand
 
its
 
solar
 
and
 
heliospheric
 
origins,
 
variation
 
in
 
plasma
 
and
 
magnetic
 
characteristics,
 
and
 
predict
 
its
 
geoeffectiveness.
 
Furthermore,
 
we
 
focus
 
on
 
bringing
 
to
 
the
 
community
 
the
 
challenges
 
and
 
limitations
 
faced
 
during
 
themodelling
 
of
 
such
 
a
 
complex,
 
yet
 
important
 
environment.
 
We
 
use
 
multi-point
 
remote
 
observations
 
of
 
CMEs
 
and
 
employ
 
different
 
methods
 
to
 
estimate
 
their
 
speeds
 
and
 
geometry.
 
These
 
parameters
 
drive
 
the
 
3D
 
magnetohydrodynamic
 
simulations
 
of
 
CME
 
evolution
 
and
 
heliospheric
 
propagation
 
in
 
the
 
framework
 
of
 
the
 
EUHFORIA
 
model.
 
The
 
best-performing
 
simulation
 
reproduced
 
the
 
arrival
 
time
 
of
 
storm
 
and
 
peak
 
intensity
 
with
 
a
 
lead
 
of
 
2
 
hours,
 
and
 
estimated
 
the
 
storm
 
strength
 
with
 
about
 
70%
 
accuracy.
 
This
 
work
 
underscores
 
the
 
requirements
 
and
 
challenges
 
involved
 
in
 
accurately
 
modelling
 
extreme
 
events.
 
The
 
major
 
bottleneck
 
was
 
to
 
constrain
 
the
 
CME
 
input
 
parameters
 
that
 
dictate
 
the
 
accuracy
 
of
 
the
 
data-driven
 
simulations.
 
Predicting
 
the
 
impacts
 
of
 
the
 
complex
 
heliospheric
 
structures
 
during
 
this
 
event
 
required
 
the
 
modelling
 
of
 
the
 
CME-CME
 
interactions
 
accurately.
 
As
 
a
 
community,
 
we
 
need
 
to
 
invest
 
more
 
in
 
observational
 
infrastructure
 
while
 
improving
 
the
 
speed
 
and
 
accuracy
 
of
 
our
 
MHD
 
forecasting
 
models.
 
 
Keywords:
 
Sun,
 
Coronal
 
mass
 
ejections
 
(CMEs),
 
Solar-terrestrial
 
relations.
 
 
1.
 
INTRODUCTION
 
Coronal
 
Mass
 
Ejections
 
(CMEs)
 
are
 
large-scale
 
expulsions
 
of
 
magnetized
 
plasma
 
from
 
the
 
solar
 
corona
 
into
 
the
 
heliosphere.
 
These
 
eruptions
 
play
 
a
 
fundamental
 
role
 
in
 
solar-terrestrial
 
interactions
 
and
 
space
 
weather
 
phenomena.
 
When
 
multiple
 
CMEs
 
are
 
ejected
 
in
 
close
 
temporal
 
proximity,
 
they
 
may
 
interact
 
dynamically
 
as
 
they
 
propagate
 
through
 
the
 
interplanetary
 
medium.
 
The
 
study
 
of
 
CME-CME
 
interactions
 
is
 
critical
 
for
 
understanding
 
their
 
kinematic
 
evolution,
 
magnetic
 
field
 
restructuring,
 
and
 
geo-effectiveness,
 
as
 
such
 
interactions
 
can
 
lead
 
to
 
enhanced
 
or
 
reduced
 
space
 
weather
 
impacts
 
depending
 
on
 
the
 
interaction
 
dynamics
 
(Gopalswamy
 
et
 
al.
 
2001,
 
Lugaz
 
et
 
al.
 
2012).
 
 
 
Nearly
 
one-third
 
of
 
CMEs
 
are
 
complex
 
CMEs
 
(Vourlidas
 
&
 
Howard,
 
2013;
 
Lugaz
 
et
 
al.
 
2017).
 
CMEs
 
accompanied
 
by
 
their
 
associated
 
interplanetary
 
components
 
(such
 
as
 
CME-driven
 
shocks,
 
sheaths,
 
and
 
magnetic
 
ejecta,
 
as
 
exemplified
 
by
 
(Kilpua
 
et
 
al.
 
2017),
 
have
 
been
 
identified
 
as
 
the
 
leading
 
cause
 
of
 
up
 
to
 
90%
 
of
 
severe
 
geomagnetic
 
storms
 
characterised
 
by
 
a
 
disturbance
 
storm
 
time
 
(Dst)
 
index
 
below
 
-100
 
nT
 
Zhang
 
et
 
al.
 
(2007).
 
Although
 
a
 
majority
 
of
 
these
 
severe
 
storms
 
can
 
be
 
attributed
 
to
 
single
 
CME
 
events
 
(approximately
 
60%),
 
a
 
noteworthy
 
proportion
 
(around
 
27%)
 
can
 
be
 
traced
 
back
 
to
 
complex
 
signatures
 
formed
 
through
 
interactions
 
between
 
individual
 
CMEs
 
and
 
other
 
transient
 
phenomena,
 
including
 
other
 
CMEs
 
and
 
regions
 
of
 
stream
 
interaction
 
(Zhang
 
et
 
al.
 
2007;
 
Vennestrøm
 
et
 
al.
 
2016).
 
 
 
It
 
is
 
important
 
to
 
highlight
 
that
 
the
 
probability
 
of
 
CME-CME
 
interactions
 
occurring
 
in
 
both
 
the
 
solar
 
corona
 
and
 
interplanetary
 
space
 
is
 
significantly
 
elevated
 
during
 
periods
 
of
 
maximum
 
solar
 
activity
 
(Rodr´ıguez
 
G´omez
 
et
 
al.
 
2020).
 
Research
 
by
 
Yashiro
 
et
 
al.
 
(2004)
 
and
 
Robbrecht
 
&
 
Berghmans
 
(2009)
 
has
 
documented
 
instances
 
in
 
which
 
the
 
rate
 
of
 
CME
 
occurrence
 
can
 
exceed
 
10
 
CMEs
 
per
 
day
 
during
 
these
 
active
 
phases.
 
Several
 
investigations
 
have
 
indicated
 
that
 
interactions
 
between
 
CMEs
 
are
 
likely
 
to
 
amplify
 
the
 
impact
 
of
 
individual
 
CMEs
 
on
 
Earth,
 
referred
 
to
 
as
 
geoeffectiveness
 
(e.g.,
 
Lugaz
 
et
 
al.,
 
2017;
 
Kilpua
 
et
 
al.,
 
2021;
 
Soni
 
et
 
al.,
 
2023).
 
However,
 
the
 
geoeffectiveness
 
amplification
 
due
 
to
 
CME-CME
 
interactions
 
is
 
not
 
yet
 
reliably
 
quantified.
 
Soni
 
et
 
al.
 
(2023);
 
Scolini
 
et
 
al.
 
(2020);
 
Maharana
 
et
 
al.
 
(2023)
 
proposed
 
that
 
the
 
geo-effectiveness
 
of
 
interacting
 
successive
 
CMEs
 
is
 
profoundly
 
amplified,
 
almost
 
twice
 
that
 
of
 
their
 
individual
 
impacts.
 
 
 
CME-CME
 
interactions
 
are
 
governed
 
by
 
a
 
range
 
of
 
factors,
 
including
 
the
 
relative
 
velocities,
 
magnetic
 
configurations,
 
and
 
longitudinal
 
separations
 
of
 
the
 
interacting
 
CMEs
 
(Maharana
 
et
 
al.
 
2023).
 
Such
 
interactions
 
can
 
result
 
in
 
mergers,
 
in
 
which
 
two
 
CMEs
 
coalesce
 
into
 
a
 
single,
 
more
 
complex
 
structure,
 
or
 
deflections,
 
in
 
which
 
one
 
CME
 
alters
 
the
 
trajectory
 
of
 
another
 
(Lugaz
 
et
 
al.
 
2017).
 
These
 
interactions
 
may
 
also
 
modify
 
the
 
magnetic
 
and
 
plasma
 
properties
 
of
 
the
 
CMEs,
 
influencing
 
their
 
geo-effectiveness
 
upon
 
arrival
 
at
 
Earth
 
(Shen
 
et
 
al.,
 
2017;
 
Temmer
 
et
 
al.,
 
2012;
 
Maharana
 
et
 
al.,
 
2023).
 
Observational
 
and
 
numerical
 
studies
 
have
 
demonstrated
 
that
 
interactions
 
between
 
CMEs
 
can
 
lead
 
to
 
acceleration
 
or
 
deceleration,
 
enhanced
 
magnetic
 
reconnection,
 
and
 
the
 
formation
 
of
 
complex
 
shock
 
structures
 
(Liu
 
et
 
al.,
 
2014;
 
Manchester
 
et
 
al.,
 
2017).
 
 
 
Interacting
 
CMEs
 
can
 
be
 
classified
 
into
 
three
 
categories
 
based
 
on
 
their
 
solar
 
source
 
regions:
 
a)
 
homologous
 
(or
 
quasi-homologous),
 
b)
 
sympathetic,
 
and
 
c)
 
analogous.
 
Homologous
 
or
 
quasi-homologous
 
CMEs
 
originate
 
from
 
the
 
same
 
active
 
region
 
and
 
are
 
frequently
 
associated
 
with
 
homologous
 
flares
 
(Schmieder
 
et
 
al.,
 
1984,
 
Svestka,
 
1989).

papers.title: Evidence of 5 minute Oscillations from Parker Solar Probe
papers.authors: Huang, Zesen, Velli, Marco, Panasenco, Olga et al.
papers.one_line_summary: Parker Solar Probe의 최근접 관측에서 태양 상층 코로나에 5분 진동의 직접적인 현장 증거를 최초로 발견하였다.
papers.background: 태양 표면의 5분 주기 진동(p-modes)은 대류층 내에 갇힌 음파에 의해 생성되는 특징적인 진동이다. 이러한 진동이 태양풍까지 도달할 것으로 오랫동안 가설화되었지만, Parker Solar Probe의 이전 근접 비행에서도 직접적인 현장 증거는 발견되지 않았다. 태양 대기의 5분 진동 전파는 태양풍 가속과 코로나 가열 메커니즘 이해에 중요하다.
papers.method: Parker Solar Probe의 3번의 최근접 근일점 관측 자료를 분석하여 상층 코로나에서의 5분 진동 신호를 탐지하였다. 플라즈마 속도, 밀도, 자기장 변화의 시계열 자료에서 5분 주기성을 통계적으로 분석하고, 태양 표면 진동과의 상관관계를 조사하였다.
papers.findings: Parker Solar Probe의 최근접 관측에서 명확한 5분 주기 진동 신호를 발견하였다. 이는 태양풍 속도와 자기장 변화에서 뚜렷하게 나타났으며, 태양 표면의 p-mode와 일치하는 특성을 보였다. 진동 진폭은 태양에서 멀어질수록 감소하는 경향을 보여 태양 기원임을 확인하였다.
papers.significance: 태양 진동이 실제로 상층 코로나와 태양풍까지 전파됨을 최초로 현장에서 직접 증명하였다. 이는 태양풍 가속과 코로나 가열 메커니즘에 대한 새로운 통찰을 제공하며, 태양-헬리오스피어 연결성 이해의 중요한 진전을 나타낸다.

papers.title: Strong Prevalence of Hammerhead Velocity Distributions Close to the Heliospheric Current Sheet
papers.authors: Srijan Bharati Das, Jaye L. Verniero, Samuel T. Badman et al.
papers.one_line_summary: Parker Solar Probe 관측에서 헬리오스피어 전류판 근처에서 망치머리 형태의 양성자 속도 분포가 우세하게 나타남을 발견하였다.
papers.background: 태양풍은 태양으로부터 멀어지면서 비단열적 가열을 겪는다. 비평형 상태의 이온 속도 위상공간 분포는 이러한 가열에 크게 기여할 수 있는 자유 에너지원을 나타낸다. Parker Solar Probe는 코어와 빔 집단 사이에 뚜렷하게 수축된 간격을 가진 고도로 이방성이고 수직으로 확산된 양성자 빔을 포함하는 속도 분포를 관측했다.
papers.method: Parker Solar Probe의 현장 관측 자료를 분석하여 헬리오스피어 전류판(Heliospheric Current Sheet, HCS) 근처에서 관측되는 특이한 양성자 속도 분포의 특성을 조사하였다. '망치머리(hammerhead)' 형태의 속도 분포 발생 빈도와 공간적 분포를 통계적으로 분석하였다.
papers.findings: 헬리오스피어 전류판 근처에서 망치머리 형태의 속도 분포가 강하게 우세하게 나타남을 확인하였다. 이러한 분포는 코어와 빔 성분 사이의 뚜렷한 간격으로 특징지어지며, 전류판과의 근접성과 명확한 상관관계를 보였다. 전류판에서 멀어질수록 이러한 분포의 발생 빈도가 감소하였다.
papers.significance: 헬리오스피어 전류판 근처의 독특한 플라즈마 역학과 입자 가속 메커니즘에 대한 새로운 통찰을 제공한다. 태양풍 가열과 입자 에너지화 과정에서 자기장 구조의 역할을 이해하는 데 중요한 관측적 증거를 제시한다.

Strong Prevalence of Hammerhead Velocity Distributions Close to the Heliospheric Current Sheet
Srijan Bharati Das
 ,1Jaye L. Verniero
 ,2Samuel T. Badman
 ,1Robert Alexander
 ,2, 3
Michael Terres
 ,1Federico Fraschetti
 ,1Kristoff W. Paulson
 ,1Fernando Carcaboso
 ,2, 3
Tatiana Niembro
 ,1Roberto Livi
 ,4Davin Larson
 ,4Ali Rahmati
 ,4Yeimy J. Rivera
 ,1Niranjana
 ,5
Kristopher G. Klein
 ,5, 6and Michael L. Stevens
1
1Center for Astrophysics — Harvard & Smithsonian, 60 Garden Street, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA
2Code 672, NASA, Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, MD 20771, USA
3GPHI, University of Maryland, Baltimore County, Baltimore, MD 21250, USA
4Space Sciences Laboratory, University of California, Berkeley, CA 94720-7450, USA
5Lunar and Planetary Laboratory, University of Arizona, Tucson, AZ 85721, USA
6Department of Planetary Sciences, University of Arizona, Tucson, AZ 85721, USA
ABSTRACT
The solar wind undergoes non-adiabatic heating as it travels away from the Sun. The velocity
phase space distribution of non-equilibrium ions in the solar wind indicate a source of free energy
that could contribute significantly to this heating. Parker Solar Probe (PSP) has observed velocity
distributions containing highly anisotropic, perpendicularly diffused proton beams with a distinctly
constricted gap between the core and beam populations. These distributions resemble a “hammerhead”
shape and were first reported in the fourth PSP encounter. Numerical simulations have reproduced
the qualitative nature of hammerheads under certain initial conditions, but have not convincingly
captured the prevalence or extreme attributes of the observed beam. This necessitates a broad study
of the occurrence conditions and the associated plasma processes to better guide simulations. We
statistically investigate the occurrence of these structures from 20 recent PSP encounters, and find
that hammerheads dominantly occur around the Heliospheric Current Sheet (HCS). As the inclination
of the HCS at PSP crossing points increases over the rising phase of the solar cycle, the occurrence
of hammerheads is increasingly concentrated in narrow time periods around the HCS crossings. For
comparison with previous work, we present statistical trends in the anisotropy of the proton beam
and its connection to the density of proton beams as well as the drift speed of the beam to the core.
Our study establishes a consistent occurrence pattern of hammerhead distributions around the HCS
indicating hammerheads are diagnostics of energization processes associated with the HCS and its
escaping wind.
Keywords:plasmas — methods: data analysis — methods: analytical — Sun: solar wind
1.INTRODUCTION
Observations show that the solar wind expands non-
adiabatically through the inner heliosphere (Parker
1963; Coleman 1968; Leer et al. 1982; Hollweg & Isen-
berg 2002). However, the pathways of energy exchange
(between electromagnetic fields and particles) to sup-
port this temperature profile remains an outstanding
challenge. Since the plasma is weakly collisional, one
of the leading theories is energization via wave-particle
srijanbdas@alumni.princeton.eduinteractions (Howes 2024; Bowen et al. 2024; Rivera
et al. 2024). In this scenario, particle velocity distri-
bution functions (VDFs) that sufficiently deviate from
local thermodynamic equilibrium (LTE) generate waves
that heat the plasma (Marsch 2012; Verscharen et al.
2019). In-situ measurements of solar wind ion VDFs
over the last few decades have revealed a wide variety of
non-LTE features such as:proton beams(Asbridge et al.
1976; Goldstein et al. 2000; Marsch et al. 1982; Alterman
et al. 2018; Verniero et al. 2020; Verniero et al. 2022),
proton temperature anisotropy(Hundhausen et al. 1967;
Marsch et al. 2004; Kasper et al. 2002; Maruca 2012;arXiv:2603.11329v1  [astro-ph.SR]  11 Mar 2026
2
Huang et al. 2020),alpha particle differential flow and
mass-proportional temperatures(Robbins et al. 1970;
Marsch et al. 1982; Neugebauer et al. 1996; Steinberg
et al. 1996; Bourouaine et al. 2013; Mostafavi et al.
2022). Minor ions have also been observed to be pref-
erentially heated and accelerated compared to protons
within the inner heliosphere (Tracy et al. 2015, 2016;
Berger et al. 2011; Rivera et al. 2025a), beyond which
Coulomb collisions likely constitute the dominant ther-
malizing effect. Investigating wave-particle interactions
from a statistically large set of high-cadence observa-
tions in the inner heliosphere is necessary to understand
the mechanisms responsible for the preferential heating
and acceleration of ions (Kasper et al. 2017; Kasper &
Klein 2019). Parker Solar Probe (PSP, Fox et al. 2016)
is taking measurements closer to the Sun than any previ-
ous spacecraft, offering a unique opportunity to observe
non-LTE features early in the solar wind expansion. The
proton beams, observed by PSP, carry free energy suffi-
cient to drive plasma instabilities, making them a prime
contributor to the solar wind energy budget.
Proton VDFs are commonly partitioned into acore
component, comprising the bulk of the solar wind, as
well as a suprathermal component termed thebeam.
The presence of a two-population beam-core ion distri-
bution in the solar wind has been regularly observed
since the launch of Imp-6 in 1971 (Feldman et al. 1973).
While past studies (such as in Klein et al. 2018) have re-
ported anisotropic proton beams, Verniero et al. (2022)
(hereafter V22) discovered the presence of a distinctly
constricted gap between the perpendicularly broadened
beam (relative to the magnetic field at the larger extent
of their energy range) and the core, resembling a “ham-
merhead” shape. V22 observed that these hammerhead
distributions were concurrent with right-hand circularly
polarized waves in the spacecraft frame. Perpendicu-
larly extended proton beams resembling hammerheads
have since been observed in subsequent studies in non-
Alfv´ enic wind (Gonz´ alez et al. 2024; Malaspina et al.
2024; Larosa et al. 2025). A few recent studies have per-
formed simulations to analyze the generation and suste-
nance of these hammerhead VDFs (Pezzini et al. 2024;
Shaaban et al. 2024; Ofman et al. 2025).
The global structure of the heliosphere is shaped by
the heliospheric current sheet (HCS), a vast, dynamic
boundary that separates sectors of the Sun’s magnetic
field lines of opposite polarity carried outward by the
solar wind (Wilcox & Ness 1965; Schulz 1973). As of
December 2025, PSP has completed its 26th encounter;
routinely cutting across the HCS (Szabo et al. 2020; Far-
gette et al. 2026). An unbiased investigation of prop-
erties of hammerhead distributions and their relationto global heliospheric processes requires robust statis-
tical observations of the occurrence regions and associ-
ated electromagnetic field behavior. In this paper, we
present an automatic detection methodology for ham-
merhead distributions which presents a consistent pat-
tern of prevalent hammerhead occurrence around the
HCS.
We classify hammerheads based on the detection of a
narrowneck1connecting the core population and the
perpendicularly extended portion of the beam popu-
lation. In Sec. 2 we outline the 1-dimensional (1D)
convolution-based hammerhead detection method, the
Pythonmodulehampy2(Bharati Das & Verniero 2026).
In Sec. 3.1, we usehampyaround perihelia of E04 -
E23 to demonstrate that hammerhead occurrences peak
around the heliospheric current sheet (HCS) crossings.
We then use the hammerheads detected byhampyto
benchmark against the results of V22 to investigate the
beam’s temperature anisotropy as a function of its frac-
tional density and its drift speed relative to the core in
Sec. 3.2. There, we also present a statistical comparison
of the relative densities of the core, neck and beam com-
ponents of hammerheads. Finally, in Sec. 4, we outline a
number of future questions and statistical
... [본문 생략]
